items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: Accenting off-beats or weak beats is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Syncopation. “Ostinato” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which term means a long sustained note, usually in the bass?
items.why: It's Pedal. “Riff” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A triplet divides one beat into how many equal parts?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
